fields.body: What is the capital of India?
fields.explanation: New Delhi is the capital and seat of government of India. It is located in the northern part of the country.

fields.body: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
fields.explanation: Mars appears red because its surface is covered with iron oxide (rust). It is the fourth planet from the Sun.

fields.body: What is 15% of 200?
fields.explanation: 15% of 200 = (15/100) ├Ś 200 = 30. Percentage means per hundred.

fields.body: Who wrote the national anthem of India?
fields.explanation: Jana Gana Mana was written by Rabindranath Tagore. It was adopted as the national anthem on January 24, 1950.

fields.body: What is the chemical symbol for water?
fields.explanation: Water is composed of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. Its chemical formula is H2O.

fields.body: What does HTML stand for?
fields.explanation: HTML is the standard language for creating web pages. It describes the structure of a web page using elements represented by tags.

fields.body: Which HTML tag is used to define the largest heading?
fields.explanation: HTML has six heading levels from <h1> (largest/most important) to <h6> (smallest). <h1> is typically used for the main page title.

fields.body: What is the correct HTML tag for inserting a line break?
fields.explanation: is a void element in HTML ŌĆö it has no closing tag and simply inserts a line break in the text flow.

fields.body: Which attribute is used to provide an alternative text for an image in HTML?
fields.explanation: The alt attribute in provides alternative text if the image cannot be displayed. It is also essential for screen readers and accessibility.

fields.body: What does the <a> tag do in HTML?
fields.explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag creates a hyperlink. The href attribute specifies the URL the link points to. Without href it is just a placeholder anchor.

fields.body: Which CSS property is used to change the text color of an element?
fields.explanation: The color property sets the foreground color of an element's text content. It accepts color names, hex codes, rgb(), hsl(), and other formats.

fields.body: What does CSS stand for?
fields.explanation: CSS is a stylesheet language used to describe the presentation of HTML documents. The 'cascading' refers to the priority scheme that determines which style rules apply.

fields.body: Which CSS selector targets an element with id='header'?
fields.explanation: The # symbol is the ID selector in CSS. IDs must be unique per page. Class selectors use a dot (.) and can be reused on multiple elements.

fields.body: What is the CSS box model?
fields.explanation: Every HTML element is a rectangular box. The box model describes how content, padding, border, and margin combine to define the element's total size and spacing.

fields.body: Which CSS property controls the space between the content and the border?
fields.explanation: Padding creates space inside the border around the content. Margin creates space outside the border. Both can be set for all four sides individually.

fields.body: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable in modern JavaScript?
fields.explanation: let declares a block-scoped variable that can be reassigned. const is also block-scoped but cannot be reassigned. var is function-scoped and is considered legacy.

fields.body: What will typeof null return in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: typeof null returning 'object' is a well-known bug in JavaScript that has existed since the language's first version. null is not actually an object but a primitive value.

fields.body: What does NaN stand for in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: NaN is a special numeric value that results from invalid arithmetic operations like 0/0 or parseInt('abc'). Interestingly, typeof NaN returns 'number'.

fields.body: Which method converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
fields.explanation: JSON.parse() deserialises a JSON string into a JavaScript value or object. The reverse operation is JSON.stringify(), which converts an object into a JSON string.

fields.body: What is the output of: console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3)?
fields.explanation: Due to floating-point precision in JavaScript, 0.1 + 0.2 equals 0.30000000000000004, not exactly 0.3. Use Math.abs(a - b) < Number.EPSILON for safe comparison.

fields.body: What is a closure in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that outer function has returned. Closures enable data privacy and function factories.

fields.body: What does the arrow function syntax look like in ES6?
fields.explanation: Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax for writing functions. They also do not have their own 'this' binding ŌĆö they inherit 'this' from the enclosing lexical context.

fields.body: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: The strict equality operator === does not perform type coercion. For example, 1 === '1' is false while 1 == '1' is true because == coerces the string to a number.

fields.body: Which array method returns a new array with each element transformed?
fields.explanation: Array.map() creates a new array by applying a callback function to each element. Unlike forEach(), it returns a new array and does not mutate the original.

fields.body: What does the 'use strict' directive do in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: Strict mode was introduced in ES5. It disallows undeclared variables, prevents duplicate parameter names, and throws errors for actions that would silently fail in sloppy mode.

fields.body: What SQL statement is used to retrieve data from a database?
fields.explanation: SELECT is the most commonly used SQL statement. Its basic syntax is: SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name WHERE condition ORDER BY column ASC/DESC.

fields.body: Which SQL clause filters rows after grouping?
fields.explanation: HAVING filters groups created by GROUP BY, similar to how WHERE filters individual rows. WHERE is applied before grouping; HAVING is applied after. Example: HAVING COUNT(*) > 5.

fields.body: What does the SQL JOIN clause do?
fields.explanation: JOIN merges rows from multiple tables using a related column (usually a foreign key). Types include INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL OUTER JOIN.

fields.body: Which SQL command removes all rows from a table without deleting the table?
fields.explanation: TRUNCATE removes all rows faster than DELETE because it does not log individual row deletions. Unlike DROP, it keeps the table structure intact. TRUNCATE cannot be rolled back in most databases.

fields.body: What is a PRIMARY KEY in SQL?
fields.explanation: A PRIMARY KEY enforces uniqueness and disallows NULL values. Each table can have only one primary key, but it can span multiple columns (composite key).

fields.body: What is a SQL INDEX used for?
fields.explanation: An index is a data structure that improves the speed of queries on a database table at the cost of additional storage and slower write operations. Think of it like a book's index.

fields.body: What does ACID stand for in database transactions?
fields.explanation: ACID properties guarantee that database transactions are processed reliably. Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing; Consistency ensures valid state; Isolation prevents concurrent interference; Durability ensures persistence.

fields.body: What is a foreign key?
fields.explanation: Foreign keys establish referential integrity between tables. They ensure a row in the child table cannot reference a non-existent row in the parent table. They can trigger cascading updates or deletes.

fields.body: Which SQL function returns the number of rows in a result set?
fields.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs. COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values in a column. COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values.

fields.body: What is a SQL VIEW?
fields.explanation: A VIEW is a stored query that behaves like a table. It does not store data itself ŌĆö it dynamically executes the underlying query when accessed. Views simplify complex queries and improve security.

fields.body: What does PHP stand for?
fields.explanation: PHP is a recursive acronym. It is a server-side scripting language designed for web development. PHP code is executed on the server and the result is sent to the browser as plain HTML.

fields.body: Which PHP function is used to connect to a MySQL database using PDO?
fields.explanation: PDO (PHP Data Objects) provides a consistent interface for accessing databases. Syntax: $pdo = new PDO('mysql:host=localhost;dbname=mydb', $user, $pass). It supports prepared statements to prevent SQL injection.

fields.body: What is the difference between include and require in PHP?
fields.explanation: Both include and require insert the content of one PHP file into another. require_once and include_once variants prevent the same file from being included multiple times.

fields.body: Which PHP superglobal contains form data submitted via POST?
fields.explanation: PHP superglobals are built-in variables always available in all scopes. $_POST holds form data sent via HTTP POST. $_GET holds URL query parameters. $_SESSION holds session data. $_SERVER holds server info.

fields.body: What function is used to hash a password securely in PHP?
fields.explanation: password_hash() creates a bcrypt hash by default and automatically includes a random salt. It should be verified using password_verify(). Never use MD5 or SHA1 for password storage.

fields.body: What is a PHP trait?
fields.explanation: Traits allow developers to reuse sets of methods across independent class hierarchies. A class can use multiple traits using the 'use' keyword, providing a form of horizontal composition.

fields.body: Which PHP keyword is used to inherit from a parent class?
fields.explanation: extends creates a child class that inherits properties and methods from the parent. PHP supports single inheritance only ŌĆö a class can extend only one parent. Interfaces allow multiple implementation contracts.

fields.body: What does the PHP keyword 'static' mean inside a class?
fields.explanation: Static members are shared across all instances of a class. They are accessed with the :: (double colon) scope resolution operator: ClassName::$property or ClassName::method().

fields.body: What is method overriding in PHP OOP?
fields.explanation: When a child class defines a method with the same name as the parent, the child's version overrides the parent's. The parent method can still be called using parent::methodName() inside the child.

fields.body: What is a PHP interface?
fields.explanation: An interface declares method signatures without implementation. A class that implements an interface must define all declared methods. Unlike abstract classes, interfaces cannot have implemented methods (before PHP 8).

fields.body: What is SQL Injection?
fields.explanation: SQL Injection occurs when user input is not sanitised and is directly embedded in SQL queries. Prevention: always use prepared statements with parameterised queries and never concatenate user input into SQL.

fields.body: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
fields.explanation: XSS allows attackers to execute scripts in a victim's browser to steal cookies, session tokens, or redirect users. Prevention: escape all output using htmlspecialchars() in PHP or equivalent in other languages.

fields.body: What is a CSRF attack?
fields.explanation: Cross-Site Request Forgery forces users to execute actions they did not intend. Prevention: use CSRF tokens ŌĆö a unique secret value included in every form and verified on the server side.

fields.body: What is the purpose of prepared statements in PHP?
fields.explanation: Prepared statements send the SQL query structure to the database first, then send the data separately. The database never confuses data for SQL code, making injection impossible regardless of input content.

fields.body: What does HTTPS provide that HTTP does not?
fields.explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data in transit between the browser and server. This prevents eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and data tampering. TLS requires an SSL certificate on the server.

fields.body: What is bcrypt and why is it used for passwords?
fields.explanation: bcrypt intentionally requires more computation than MD5 or SHA1, making brute-force and rainbow table attacks impractical. Its work factor can be increased as hardware improves. PHP uses it by default in password_hash().

fields.body: What is a session fixation attack?
fields.explanation: In session fixation, the attacker sets a session ID before the user logs in, then waits for the user to authenticate. Prevention: always regenerate the session ID using session_regenerate_id(true) after a successful login.

fields.body: What is the purpose of the httpOnly cookie flag?
fields.explanation: When a cookie is set with HttpOnly, it cannot be read by JavaScript (document.cookie). This mitigates XSS attacks from stealing session cookies. The Secure flag additionally restricts cookies to HTTPS connections only.

fields.body: What is rate limiting in web security?
fields.explanation: Rate limiting prevents brute-force attacks on login forms, API abuse, and denial-of-service attempts. Implementation: track attempts by IP or user ID and temporarily block after a threshold is exceeded.

fields.body: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
fields.explanation: 2FA requires something you know (password) and something you have (phone OTP, hardware key) or something you are (biometric). Even if a password is stolen, the second factor prevents unauthorized access.

fields.explanation: TRUNCATE removes all rows faster than DELETE because it does not log individual row deletions. Unlike DROP, it keeps the table structure intact. TRUNCATE cannot be rolled back in most databases.

fields.body: What is a PRIMARY KEY in SQL?
fields.explanation: A PRIMARY KEY enforces uniqueness and disallows NULL values. Each table can have only one primary key, but it can span multiple columns (composite key).

fields.body: What is a SQL INDEX used for?
fields.explanation: An index is a data structure that improves the speed of queries on a database table at the cost of additional storage and slower write operations. Think of it like a book's index.

fields.body: What does ACID stand for in database transactions?
fields.explanation: ACID properties guarantee that database transactions are processed reliably. Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing; Consistency ensures valid state; Isolation prevents concurrent interference; Durability ensures persistence.

fields.body: What is a foreign key?
fields.explanation: Foreign keys establish referential integrity between tables. They ensure a row in the child table cannot reference a non-existent row in the parent table. They can trigger cascading updates or deletes.

fields.body: Which SQL function returns the number of rows in a result set?
fields.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs. COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values in a column. COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values.

fields.body: What is a SQL VIEW?
fields.explanation: A VIEW is a stored query that behaves like a table. It does not store data itself ŌĆö it dynamically executes the underlying query when accessed. Views simplify complex queries and improve security.

fields.body: What does PHP stand for?
fields.explanation: PHP is a recursive acronym. It is a server-side scripting language designed for web development. PHP code is executed on the server and the result is sent to the browser as plain HTML.

fields.body: Which PHP function is used to connect to a MySQL database using PDO?
fields.explanation: PDO (PHP Data Objects) provides a consistent interface for accessing databases. Syntax: $pdo = new PDO('mysql:host=localhost;dbname=mydb', $user, $pass). It supports prepared statements to prevent SQL injection.

fields.body: What is the difference between include and require in PHP?
fields.explanation: Both include and require insert the content of one PHP file into another. require_once and include_once variants prevent the same file from being included multiple times.

fields.body: Which PHP superglobal contains form data submitted via POST?
fields.explanation: PHP superglobals are built-in variables always available in all scopes. $_POST holds form data sent via HTTP POST. $_GET holds URL query parameters. $_SESSION holds session data. $_SERVER holds server info.

fields.body: What function is used to hash a password securely in PHP?
fields.explanation: password_hash() creates a bcrypt hash by default and automatically includes a random salt. It should be verified using password_verify(). Never use MD5 or SHA1 for password storage.

fields.body: What is a PHP trait?
fields.explanation: Traits allow developers to reuse sets of methods across independent class hierarchies. A class can use multiple traits using the 'use' keyword, providing a form of horizontal composition.

fields.body: Which PHP keyword is used to inherit from a parent class?
fields.explanation: extends creates a child class that inherits properties and methods from the parent. PHP supports single inheritance only ŌĆö a class can extend only one parent. Interfaces allow multiple implementation contracts.

fields.body: What does the PHP keyword 'static' mean inside a class?
fields.explanation: Static members are shared across all instances of a class. They are accessed with the :: (double colon) scope resolution operator: ClassName::$property or ClassName::method().

fields.body: What is method overriding in PHP OOP?
fields.explanation: When a child class defines a method with the same name as the parent, the child's version overrides the parent's. The parent method can still be called using parent::methodName() inside the child.

fields.body: What is a PHP interface?
fields.explanation: An interface declares method signatures without implementation. A class that implements an interface must define all declared methods. Unlike abstract classes, interfaces cannot have implemented methods (before PHP 8).

fields.body: What is SQL Injection?
fields.explanation: SQL Injection occurs when user input is not sanitised and is directly embedded in SQL queries. Prevention: always use prepared statements with parameterised queries and never concatenate user input into SQL.

fields.body: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
fields.explanation: XSS allows attackers to execute scripts in a victim's browser to steal cookies, session tokens, or redirect users. Prevention: escape all output using htmlspecialchars() in PHP or equivalent in other languages.

fields.body: What is a CSRF attack?
fields.explanation: Cross-Site Request Forgery forces users to execute actions they did not intend. Prevention: use CSRF tokens ŌĆö a unique secret value included in every form and verified on the server side.

fields.body: What is the purpose of prepared statements in PHP?
fields.explanation: Prepared statements send the SQL query structure to the database first, then send the data separately. The database never confuses data for SQL code, making injection impossible regardless of input content.

fields.body: What does HTTPS provide that HTTP does not?
fields.explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data in transit between the browser and server. This prevents eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and data tampering. TLS requires an SSL certificate on the server.

fields.body: What is bcrypt and why is it used for passwords?
fields.explanation: bcrypt intentionally requires more computation than MD5 or SHA1, making brute-force and rainbow table attacks impractical. Its work factor can be increased as hardware improves. PHP uses it by default in password_hash().

fields.body: What is a session fixation attack?
fields.explanation: In session fixation, the attacker sets a session ID before the user logs in, then waits for the user to authenticate. Prevention: always regenerate the session ID using session_regenerate_id(true) after a successful login.

fields.body: What is the purpose of the httpOnly cookie flag?
fields.explanation: When a cookie is set with HttpOnly, it cannot be read by JavaScript (document.cookie). This mitigates XSS attacks from stealing session cookies. The Secure flag additionally restricts cookies to HTTPS connections only.

fields.body: What is rate limiting in web security?
fields.explanation: Rate limiting prevents brute-force attacks on login forms, API abuse, and denial-of-service attempts. Implementation: track attempts by IP or user ID and temporarily block after a threshold is exceeded.

fields.body: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
fields.explanation: 2FA requires something you know (password) and something you have (phone OTP, hardware key) or something you are (biometric). Even if a password is stolen, the second factor prevents unauthorized access.

fields.body: What does HTML stand for?
fields.explanation: HTML is the standard language for creating web pages. It describes the structure of a web page using elements represented by tags.

fields.body: Which HTML tag is used to define the largest heading?
fields.explanation: HTML has six heading levels from <h1> (largest/most important) to <h6> (smallest). <h1> is typically used for the main page title.

fields.body: What is the correct HTML tag for inserting a line break?
fields.explanation: is a void element in HTML ŌĆö it has no closing tag and simply inserts a line break in the text flow.

fields.body: Which attribute is used to provide an alternative text for an image in HTML?
fields.explanation: The alt attribute in provides alternative text if the image cannot be displayed. It is also essential for screen readers and accessibility.

fields.body: What does the <a> tag do in HTML?
fields.explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag creates a hyperlink. The href attribute specifies the URL the link points to. Without href it is just a placeholder anchor.

fields.body: Which CSS property is used to change the text color of an element?
fields.explanation: The color property sets the foreground color of an element's text content. It accepts color names, hex codes, rgb(), hsl(), and other formats.

fields.body: What does CSS stand for?
fields.explanation: CSS is a stylesheet language used to describe the presentation of HTML documents. The 'cascading' refers to the priority scheme that determines which style rules apply.

fields.body: Which CSS selector targets an element with id='header'?
fields.explanation: The # symbol is the ID selector in CSS. IDs must be unique per page. Class selectors use a dot (.) and can be reused on multiple elements.

fields.body: What is the CSS box model?
fields.explanation: Every HTML element is a rectangular box. The box model describes how content, padding, border, and margin combine to define the element's total size and spacing.

fields.body: Which CSS property controls the space between the content and the border?
fields.explanation: Padding creates space inside the border around the content. Margin creates space outside the border. Both can be set for all four sides individually.

fields.body: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable in modern JavaScript?
fields.explanation: let declares a block-scoped variable that can be reassigned. const is also block-scoped but cannot be reassigned. var is function-scoped and is considered legacy.

fields.body: What will typeof null return in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: typeof null returning 'object' is a well-known bug in JavaScript that has existed since the language's first version. null is not actually an object but a primitive value.

fields.body: What does NaN stand for in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: NaN is a special numeric value that results from invalid arithmetic operations like 0/0 or parseInt('abc'). Interestingly, typeof NaN returns 'number'.

fields.body: Which method converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
fields.explanation: JSON.parse() deserialises a JSON string into a JavaScript value or object. The reverse operation is JSON.stringify(), which converts an object into a JSON string.

fields.body: What is the output of: console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3)?
fields.explanation: Due to floating-point precision in JavaScript, 0.1 + 0.2 equals 0.30000000000000004, not exactly 0.3. Use Math.abs(a - b) < Number.EPSILON for safe comparison.

fields.body: What is a closure in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that outer function has returned. Closures enable data privacy and function factories.

fields.body: What does the arrow function syntax look like in ES6?
fields.explanation: Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax for writing functions. They also do not have their own 'this' binding ŌĆö they inherit 'this' from the enclosing lexical context.

fields.body: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: The strict equality operator === does not perform type coercion. For example, 1 === '1' is false while 1 == '1' is true because == coerces the string to a number.

fields.body: Which array method returns a new array with each element transformed?
fields.explanation: Array.map() creates a new array by applying a callback function to each element. Unlike forEach(), it returns a new array and does not mutate the original.

fields.body: What does the 'use strict' directive do in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: Strict mode was introduced in ES5. It disallows undeclared variables, prevents duplicate parameter names, and throws errors for actions that would silently fail in sloppy mode.

fields.body: Which CSS selector targets an element with id='header'?
fields.explanation: The # symbol is the ID selector in CSS. IDs must be unique per page. Class selectors use a dot (.) and can be reused on multiple elements.

fields.body: What is the CSS box model?
fields.explanation: Every HTML element is a rectangular box. The box model describes how content, padding, border, and margin combine to define the element's total size and spacing.

fields.body: Which CSS property controls the space between the content and the border?
fields.explanation: Padding creates space inside the border around the content. Margin creates space outside the border. Both can be set for all four sides individually.

fields.body: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable in modern JavaScript?
fields.explanation: let declares a block-scoped variable that can be reassigned. const is also block-scoped but cannot be reassigned. var is function-scoped and is considered legacy.

fields.body: What will typeof null return in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: typeof null returning 'object' is a well-known bug in JavaScript that has existed since the language's first version. null is not actually an object but a primitive value.

fields.body: What does NaN stand for in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: NaN is a special numeric value that results from invalid arithmetic operations like 0/0 or parseInt('abc'). Interestingly, typeof NaN returns 'number'.

fields.body: Which method converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
fields.explanation: JSON.parse() deserialises a JSON string into a JavaScript value or object. The reverse operation is JSON.stringify(), which converts an object into a JSON string.

fields.body: What is the output of: console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3)?
fields.explanation: Due to floating-point precision in JavaScript, 0.1 + 0.2 equals 0.30000000000000004, not exactly 0.3. Use Math.abs(a - b) < Number.EPSILON for safe comparison.

fields.body: What is a closure in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that outer function has returned. Closures enable data privacy and function factories.

fields.body: What does the arrow function syntax look like in ES6?
fields.explanation: Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax for writing functions. They also do not have their own 'this' binding ŌĆö they inherit 'this' from the enclosing lexical context.

fields.body: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: The strict equality operator === does not perform type coercion. For example, 1 === '1' is false while 1 == '1' is true because == coerces the string to a number.

fields.body: Which array method returns a new array with each element transformed?
fields.explanation: Array.map() creates a new array by applying a callback function to each element. Unlike forEach(), it returns a new array and does not mutate the original.

fields.body: What does the 'use strict' directive do in JavaScript?
fields.explanation: Strict mode was introduced in ES5. It disallows undeclared variables, prevents duplicate parameter names, and throws errors for actions that would silently fail in sloppy mode.

fields.body: What SQL statement is used to retrieve data from a database?
fields.explanation: SELECT is the most commonly used SQL statement. Its basic syntax is: SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name WHERE condition ORDER BY column ASC/DESC.

fields.body: Which SQL clause filters rows after grouping?
fields.explanation: HAVING filters groups created by GROUP BY, similar to how WHERE filters individual rows. WHERE is applied before grouping; HAVING is applied after. Example: HAVING COUNT(*) > 5.

fields.body: What does the SQL JOIN clause do?
fields.explanation: JOIN merges rows from multiple tables using a related column (usually a foreign key). Types include INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL OUTER JOIN.

fields.body: Which SQL command removes all rows from a table without deleting the table?
fields.explanation: TRUNCATE removes all rows faster than DELETE because it does not log individual row deletions. Unlike DROP, it keeps the table structure intact. TRUNCATE cannot be rolled back in most databases.

fields.body: What is a PRIMARY KEY in SQL?
fields.explanation: A PRIMARY KEY enforces uniqueness and disallows NULL values. Each table can have only one primary key, but it can span multiple columns (composite key).

fields.body: What is a SQL INDEX used for?
fields.explanation: An index is a data structure that improves the speed of queries on a database table at the cost of additional storage and slower write operations. Think of it like a book's index.

fields.body: What does ACID stand for in database transactions?
fields.explanation: ACID properties guarantee that database transactions are processed reliably. Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing; Consistency ensures valid state; Isolation prevents concurrent interference; Durability ensures persistence.

fields.body: What is a foreign key?
fields.explanation: Foreign keys establish referential integrity between tables. They ensure a row in the child table cannot reference a non-existent row in the parent table. They can trigger cascading updates or deletes.

fields.body: Which SQL function returns the number of rows in a result set?
fields.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs. COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values in a column. COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values.

fields.body: What is a SQL VIEW?
fields.explanation: A VIEW is a stored query that behaves like a table. It does not store data itself ŌĆö it dynamically executes the underlying query when accessed. Views simplify complex queries and improve security.

fields.body: What does PHP stand for?
fields.explanation: PHP is a recursive acronym. It is a server-side scripting language designed for web development. PHP code is executed on the server and the result is sent to the browser as plain HTML.

fields.body: Which PHP function is used to connect to a MySQL database using PDO?
fields.explanation: PDO (PHP Data Objects) provides a consistent interface for accessing databases. Syntax: $pdo = new PDO('mysql:host=localhost;dbname=mydb', $user, $pass). It supports prepared statements to prevent SQL injection.

fields.body: What is the difference between include and require in PHP?
fields.explanation: Both include and require insert the content of one PHP file into another. require_once and include_once variants prevent the same file from being included multiple times.

fields.body: Which PHP superglobal contains form data submitted via POST?
fields.explanation: PHP superglobals are built-in variables always available in all scopes. $_POST holds form data sent via HTTP POST. $_GET holds URL query parameters. $_SESSION holds session data. $_SERVER holds server info.

fields.body: What function is used to hash a password securely in PHP?
fields.explanation: password_hash() creates a bcrypt hash by default and automatically includes a random salt. It should be verified using password_verify(). Never use MD5 or SHA1 for password storage.

fields.body: What is a PHP trait?
fields.explanation: Traits allow developers to reuse sets of methods across independent class hierarchies. A class can use multiple traits using the 'use' keyword, providing a form of horizontal composition.

fields.body: Which PHP keyword is used to inherit from a parent class?
fields.explanation: extends creates a child class that inherits properties and methods from the parent. PHP supports single inheritance only ŌĆö a class can extend only one parent. Interfaces allow multiple implementation contracts.

fields.body: What does the PHP keyword 'static' mean inside a class?
fields.explanation: Static members are shared across all instances of a class. They are accessed with the :: (double colon) scope resolution operator: ClassName::$property or ClassName::method().

fields.body: What is method overriding in PHP OOP?
fields.explanation: When a child class defines a method with the same name as the parent, the child's version overrides the parent's. The parent method can still be called using parent::methodName() inside the child.

fields.body: What is a PHP interface?
fields.explanation: An interface declares method signatures without implementation. A class that implements an interface must define all declared methods. Unlike abstract classes, interfaces cannot have implemented methods (before PHP 8).

fields.body: What is SQL Injection?
fields.explanation: SQL Injection occurs when user input is not sanitised and is directly embedded in SQL queries. Prevention: always use prepared statements with parameterised queries and never concatenate user input into SQL.

fields.body: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
fields.explanation: XSS allows attackers to execute scripts in a victim's browser to steal cookies, session tokens, or redirect users. Prevention: escape all output using htmlspecialchars() in PHP or equivalent in other languages.

fields.body: What is a CSRF attack?
fields.explanation: Cross-Site Request Forgery forces users to execute actions they did not intend. Prevention: use CSRF tokens ŌĆö a unique secret value included in every form and verified on the server side.

fields.body: What is the purpose of prepared statements in PHP?
fields.explanation: Prepared statements send the SQL query structure to the database first, then send the data separately. The database never confuses data for SQL code, making injection impossible regardless of input content.

fields.body: What does HTTPS provide that HTTP does not?
fields.explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data in transit between the browser and server. This prevents eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and data tampering. TLS requires an SSL certificate on the server.

fields.body: What is bcrypt and why is it used for passwords?
fields.explanation: bcrypt intentionally requires more computation than MD5 or SHA1, making brute-force and rainbow table attacks impractical. Its work factor can be increased as hardware improves. PHP uses it by default in password_hash().

fields.body: What is a session fixation attack?
fields.explanation: In session fixation, the attacker sets a session ID before the user logs in, then waits for the user to authenticate. Prevention: always regenerate the session ID using session_regenerate_id(true) after a successful login.

fields.body: What is the purpose of the httpOnly cookie flag?
fields.explanation: When a cookie is set with HttpOnly, it cannot be read by JavaScript (document.cookie). This mitigates XSS attacks from stealing session cookies. The Secure flag additionally restricts cookies to HTTPS connections only.

fields.body: What is rate limiting in web security?
fields.explanation: Rate limiting prevents brute-force attacks on login forms, API abuse, and denial-of-service attempts. Implementation: track attempts by IP or user ID and temporarily block after a threshold is exceeded.

fields.body: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
fields.explanation: 2FA requires something you know (password) and something you have (phone OTP, hardware key) or something you are (biometric). Even if a password is stolen, the second factor prevents unauthorized access.

fields.body: What does HTML stand for?
fields.explanation: HTML is the standard language for creating web pages. It describes the structure of a web page using elements represented by tags.

fields.body: Which HTML tag is used to define the largest heading?
fields.explanation: HTML has six heading levels from <h1> (largest/most important) to <h6> (smallest). <h1> is typically used for the main page title.

fields.body: What is the correct HTML tag for inserting a line break?
fields.explanation: is a void element in HTML ŌĆö it has no closing tag and simply inserts a line break in the text flow.

fields.body: Which attribute is used to provide an alternative text for an image in HTML?
fields.explanation: The alt attribute in provides alternative text if the image cannot be displayed. It is also essential for screen readers and accessibility.

fields.body: What does the <a> tag do in HTML?
fields.explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag creates a hyperlink. The href attribute specifies the URL the link points to. Without href it is just a placeholder anchor.

fields.explanation: The color property sets the foreground color of an element's text content. It accepts color names, hex codes, rgb(), hsl(), and other formats.

fields.body: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
fields.explanation: XSS allows attackers to execute scripts in a victim's browser to steal cookies, session tokens, or redirect users. Prevention: escape all output using htmlspecialchars() in PHP or equivalent in other languages.

fields.body: What is a CSRF attack?
fields.explanation: Cross-Site Request Forgery forces users to execute actions they did not intend. Prevention: use CSRF tokens ŌĆö a unique secret value included in every form and verified on the server side.

fields.body: What is the purpose of prepared statements in PHP?
fields.explanation: Prepared statements send the SQL query structure to the database first, then send the data separately. The database never confuses data for SQL code, making injection impossible regardless of input content.

fields.body: What does HTTPS provide that HTTP does not?
fields.explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data in transit between the browser and server. This prevents eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and data tampering. TLS requires an SSL certificate on the server.

fields.body: What is bcrypt and why is it used for passwords?
fields.explanation: bcrypt intentionally requires more computation than MD5 or SHA1, making brute-force and rainbow table attacks impractical. Its work factor can be increased as hardware improves. PHP uses it by default in password_hash().

fields.body: What is a session fixation attack?
fields.explanation: In session fixation, the attacker sets a session ID before the user logs in, then waits for the user to authenticate. Prevention: always regenerate the session ID using session_regenerate_id(true) after a successful login.

fields.body: What is the purpose of the httpOnly cookie flag?
fields.explanation: When a cookie is set with HttpOnly, it cannot be read by JavaScript (document.cookie). This mitigates XSS attacks from stealing session cookies. The Secure flag additionally restricts cookies to HTTPS connections only.

fields.body: What is rate limiting in web security?
fields.explanation: Rate limiting prevents brute-force attacks on login forms, API abuse, and denial-of-service attempts. Implementation: track attempts by IP or user ID and temporarily block after a threshold is exceeded.

fields.body: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
fields.explanation: 2FA requires something you know (password) and something you have (phone OTP, hardware key) or something you are (biometric). Even if a password is stolen, the second factor prevents unauthorized access.
